name: Impura
classification_notes: Impura is a display typeface with a distinctly underground, expressive character, evoked through its sample copy referencing punk, rebellion and DIY culture, placing it firmly in the display category with an expressive subcategory.
summary: An eight-weight display family from Fuerte Type with a raw, underground character and an extensive set of OpenType alternates and stylistic sets.
description: Spanning eight weights from ExtraThin to Black, Impura is aimed at editorial, poster and identity work that leans into subculture and countercultural aesthetics. Nine stylistic sets, swashes, contextual alternates and discretionary ligatures give designers considerable room to vary texture and voice. The sample copy, referencing punk basements, guerrilla galleries and DIY spaces, signals the intended register clearly.
foundry_blurb: Fuerte Type positions Impura as a typeface for the underground and the rebellious, framing creativity as a form of radical self-determination and resistance against mainstream aesthetics.

name: Pilar
classification_notes: Pilar is explicitly influenced by mid-20th century Swiss Neo-Grotesque typefaces, with clean geometric forms and a rational structure softened by subtle humanist details.
summary: A Swiss Neo-Grotesque with seven weights and matching italics, balancing rational clarity with subtle warmth for extended reading and editorial use.
description: Influenced by mid-20th century Swiss grotesques, Pilar keeps clean, simple letterforms while introducing small details that prevent it from reading as purely mechanical. The family runs from Hair to Black with italic counterparts throughout, and supports a comprehensive OpenType feature set including discretionary ligatures, fractions, case-sensitive forms and multiple numeral styles. It sits comfortably across both long-form text and display settings.
foundry_blurb: Fuerte Type describe Pilar as a straightforward, highly useful sans-serif with clean simple shapes, timeless aesthetics and harmonious texture suited to long-form reading. They note the family draws on the rationality of classic Swiss Neo-Grotesque typefaces from the mid-20th century, balanced with subtle quirks and details that introduce warmth and give it a distinct presence among contemporary sans-serifs.

name: Brick
classification_notes: Brick is presented in distinct Display and Text optical subfamilies, suggesting a high-contrast serif with editorial and display applications in mind, consistent with a contemporary display serif classification.
summary: A serif family from Fuerte Type split into Display and Text optical cuts, each running across five weights from Thin to Black, with a distinctly British pub and brewing sensibility in its specimen copy.
description: Brick divides into two purpose-built subfamilies: Brick Display for large-scale setting and Brick Text for smaller, continuous reading, each sharing five weights from Thin to Black. The specimen leans heavily on East London pub culture, brewery history and Sunday roast copy, giving the family a clear sense of its intended territory. An extensive OpenType feature set covers small caps, old-style figures, historical forms, ornaments and thirteen stylistic sets, offering considerable flexibility for editorial and identity work.
foundry_blurb: Fuerte Type present Brick as a serif family in two optical cuts, Display and Text, each available in five weights. The specimen copy draws on London brewing history, pub signage and British food culture, positioning the typeface for brand and editorial contexts with strong cultural character.

name: Sakral
classification_notes: Sakral presents as a serif with Display and Text optical subfamilies, suggesting classical proportions adapted for both large-scale and body use, consistent with a transitional or contemporary serif classification.
summary: A serif family split into Display and Text optical sizes, each running across four weights with matching italics for editorial and publication use.
description: Sakral divides into two purpose-built subfamilies: Sakral Display for large settings and Sakral Text for body copy, each offering Regular, Medium, Bold and Black with corresponding italics. The family carries an extensive OpenType feature set including small caps, old-style figures, historical forms, ornaments and fractions, making it well suited to long-form typesetting. Sample settings in German, Spanish and English suggest broad Latin coverage and a design sensibility oriented towards cultural and editorial contexts.
foundry_blurb: Fuerte Type present Sakral as a two-subfamily serif covering Display and Text optical sizes, with four weights and italics in each, supported by a full suite of OpenType features for sophisticated typographic work.

name: Thesaurus
classification_notes: The family's two optical subfamilies — Text and Display — alongside its historical forms, swashes and old-style figures point to a classical serif tradition rooted in transitional or early modern letterforms, with the Display cuts showing the contrast and refinement typical of that lineage.
summary: A two-subfamily serif family from Fuerte Type, split into Text and Display optical cuts across four weights with extensive OpenType features including historical forms, swashes and old-style figures.
description: Spanning eight styles each in Text and Display subfamilies, this family is built for the full range of typographic scale from continuous reading to large-scale headlines. The feature set is notably thorough, covering historical forms, historical ligatures, swashes, small caps, ornaments and multiple figure styles. The classical sensibility of the letterforms, particularly evident in the Display italics, suggests a typeface well suited to editorial, cultural and literary publishing work.
foundry_blurb: Fuerte Type present Thesaurus as a serif family in two optical cuts — Text and Display — each available in Regular, Medium, Bold and Black with matching italics. The family ships with an extensive OpenType feature set including swashes, historical forms, ornaments, small caps and multiple numeral styles.
